Model Details

Appendix_Model.Rmd

BRIER.FULL

The BRIER.FULL procedure aims to minimize the following objective function:

\[ Q_{\mathbf{\eta}}(\mathbf{\beta}) = -\, \ell\!\left(\mathbf{\beta}\mid \mathbf{X}, \mathbf{Y}\right) - \sum_{m=1}^{M} \eta_m\, \ell\!\left(\mathbf{\beta}\mid \check{\mathbf{X}}_m, \check{\mathbf{Y}}_m\right). \]

The integration weight \(\eta_m \ge 0\) controls the extent of information transferred from the \(m\)-th external cohort to the target data. Here, \(\ell(\cdot)\) denotes the log-likelihood function. For generalized linear models and the Cox proportional hazards model, the corresponding log-likelihood functions are specified below.

Generalized Linear Models

Assume that the conditional distribution of \(Y_i\) given \(\mathbf{X}_i\) belongs to the canonical exponential family, with conditional density

\[ f(Y_i; \theta_i, \phi) \propto \exp\left( \frac{Y_i \theta_i - b(\theta_i)}{a(\phi)} \right), \]

where \(a(\cdot)\) and \(b(\cdot)\) are pre-specified functions corresponding to the assumed outcome distribution, \(\phi\) is a nuisance parameter related to the variance, and \(\theta_i\) is the canonical parameter. The mean and variance satisfy

\[ \mu_i = \mathbb{E}(Y_i \mid \theta_i) = b'(\theta_i), \]

and

\[ \mathrm{Var}(Y_i \mid \theta_i) = a(\phi)\, b''(\theta_i). \]

The mean \(\mu_i\) is related to the linear predictor through a link function:

\[ g(\mu_i) = \eta_i = \mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{\beta}. \]

The log-likelihood function is given by

\[ \ell(\mathbf{\beta}) = \sum_{i=1}^n \log f\!\left(y_i \mid \theta_i(\mathbf{\beta}), \phi\right) = \sum_{i=1}^n \left[ \frac{y_i \theta_i(\mathbf{\beta}) - b\!\left(\theta_i(\mathbf{\beta})\right)}{a(\phi)} + c(y_i,\phi) \right]. \]

(#tab:glm-family) Exponential-family distributions implemented in BRIER2, including cumulant functions \(b(\theta)\) and canonical link functions \(g(\mu)\).
Family \(\mathbf{b(\theta)}\) \(\mathbf{\mu=b'(\theta)}\) \(\mathbf{\mathrm{Var}(Y)}\) \(g(\mu)\)
Gaussian \(\dfrac{\theta^2}{2}\) \(\theta\) \(\phi\) \(\mu\)
Bernoulli \(\log(1+e^{\theta})\) \(\dfrac{e^\theta}{1+e^\theta}\) \(\mu(1-\mu)\) \(\log\!\left(\dfrac{\mu}{1-\mu}\right)\)
Poisson \(e^{\theta}\) \(e^\theta\) \(\mu\) \(\log(\mu)\)
Negative Binomial \(-r\log(1-e^{\theta}),\;\theta<0\) \(\dfrac{r e^\theta}{1-e^\theta}\) \(\mu+\dfrac{\mu^2}{r}\) \(\log\!\left(\dfrac{\mu}{\mu+r}\right)\)

Cox Proportional Hazards Models

For the Cox proportional hazard model, the negative log-partial likelihood admits a KL-divergence interpretation through the sequence of conditional failure-time distributions. Let \(t_{(1)} < \cdots < t_{(K)}\) denote the ordered distinct event times, and let \(\mathcal{R}_k\) denote the risk set immediately prior to \(t_{(k)}\). If subject \(i_k\) experiences the event at \(t_{(k)}\), the working Cox model induces the conditional probability

\[ f^{(\mathbf{\beta})}(i_k \mid \mathcal{R}_k) = \frac{\exp(\mathbf{x}_{i_k}^{\top}\mathbf{\beta})} {\sum_{j\in\mathcal{R}_k}\exp(\mathbf{x}_j^{\top}\mathbf{\beta})}. \]

The log-partial likelihood of the working Cox model is

\[ \ell_p(\mathbf{\beta}) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \log f^{(\mathbf{\beta})}(i_k \mid \mathcal{R}_k) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \left[ \mathbf{x}_{i_k}^{\top}\mathbf{\beta} - \log \left( \sum_{j\in\mathcal{R}_k} \exp(\mathbf{x}_j^{\top}\mathbf{\beta}) \right) \right]. \]

BRIER.I

The BRIER.I procedure minimizes the following objective:

\[ Q_{\mathbf{\eta}}(\mathbf{\beta}) = -\, \ell\!\left(\mathbf{\beta}\mid \mathbf{X}, \mathbf{Y}\right) + \sum_{m=1}^{M} \eta_m\, \operatorname{D_{KL}}\!\left(\check{f}_m \,\|\, f\right). \]

The integration weight \(\eta_m \ge 0\) controls the extent of information transfer from the \(m\)-th external cohort to the target data. Here, \(\ell\!\left(\mathbf{\beta}\mid \mathbf{X}, \mathbf{Y}\right)\) is the log-likelihood evaluated on the target individual-level data, and \(\operatorname{D_{KL}}\!\left(\check{f}_m \,\|\, f\right)\) is the Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence (kullback1951_kl?), measuring the discrepancy between the density \(\check{f}_m\) induced by the external model \(\check{\mathbf{\beta}}_m\) and the density \(f\) induced by the target working model \(\mathbf{\beta}\). For generalized linear models and the Cox proportional hazards model, the corresponding KL divergences are specified below.

Generalized Linear Models

For outcomes following an exponential-family distribution, BRIER.I follows the prior lasso framework of (jiang2016_priorlasso?) to quantify the discrepancy between the target working model and external information. For an individual \(i\) in the target cohort, let \(f\) denote the density defined by the target model:

\[ f(y_i) \propto \exp\left\{ \frac{y_i \theta_i - b(\theta_i)}{a(\phi)} \right\}, \]

where \(b(\theta)\) is the cumulant function and \(g(\cdot)\) is the link function. The canonical parameter \(\theta_i\) satisfies

\[ b'(\theta_i) = g^{-1}(\mathbf{X}_i^\top \mathbf{\beta}). \]

The density \(\check{f}_{i,m}\) is induced by the \(m\)-th external model, with canonical parameter \(\check{\theta}_{i,m}\) satisfying

\[ b'(\check{\theta}_{i,m}) = g^{-1}(\mathbf{X}_i^\top \check{\mathbf{\beta}}_m). \]

The Kullback-Leibler divergence between the external model and the working model is defined as

\[ D_{\mathrm{KL}}\!\left(\check{f}_m \,\|\, f\right) = \mathbb{E}_{\check{f}_m} \left( \log \frac{\check{f}_m}{f} \right). \]

Cox Proportional Hazards Models

For time-to-event outcomes, BRIER.I adopts the CoxKL framework of (wang2023_coxkl?) to construct a KL divergence based on the Cox partial likelihood. We formulate the censored survival data as a dynamic ranking problem over \(S\) unique failure times \(t_1 < \dots < t_S\). At each event time \(t_s\), let \(f(A_s \mid B_s)\) denote the conditional density defined by the target model:

\[ f(A_s \mid B_s) = \frac{\exp\{\mathbf{X}_s^\top \mathbf{\beta}\}} {\sum_{i=1}^n Y_i(t_s)\exp\{\mathbf{X}_i^\top \mathbf{\beta}\}}, \]

and let \(\check{f}_m(A_s \mid B_s)\) denote the corresponding density induced by the \(m\)-th external risk score \(\mathbf{X}^\top \check{\mathbf{\beta}}_m\):

\[ \check{f}_m(A_s \mid B_s) = \frac{\exp\{\mathbf{X}_s^\top \check{\mathbf{\beta}}_m\}} {\sum_{i=1}^n Y_i(t_s)\exp\{\mathbf{X}_i^\top \check{\mathbf{\beta}}_m\}}. \]

The discrepancy between the target model and the \(m\)-th external model is measured by the accumulated KL divergence across all conditional experiments:

\[ D_{\mathrm{KL}}(\check{f}_m \parallel f) = \sum_{s=1}^S \mathbb{E}_{\check{f}_m} \left[ \log \frac{\check{f}_m(A_s \mid B_s)} {f(A_s \mid B_s)} \right]. \]

BRIER.S

The BRIER.S procedure minimizes the following objective function, constructed from summary-level data using the squared Mahalanobis distance:

\[ Q_{\mathbf{\eta}}(\mathbf{\beta}) = \frac{1}{2}\, \operatorname{D}_{\widetilde{\mathbf{\Sigma}}} \left( \widetilde{\mathbf{\Sigma}}^{-1}\widetilde{\mathbf{r}}\, \|\, \mathbf{\beta} \right) + \frac{1}{2} \sum_{m=1}^{M} \eta_m\, \operatorname{D}_{\widetilde{\mathbf{\Sigma}}} \left( \check{\mathbf{\beta}}_m\, \|\, \mathbf{\beta} \right). \]

The integration weight \(\eta_m \ge 0\) controls the contribution of each external model. The first term,

\[ \operatorname{D}_{\widetilde{\mathbf{\Sigma}}} \left( \widetilde{\mathbf{\Sigma}}^{-1}\widetilde{\mathbf{r}}\, \|\, \mathbf{\beta} \right), \]

is the squared Mahalanobis distance measuring the discrepancy between the target summary-level data and the target working model. The second term,

\[ \operatorname{D}_{\widetilde{\mathbf{\Sigma}}} \left( \check{\mathbf{\beta}}_m\, \|\, \mathbf{\beta} \right), \]

measures the discrepancy between the target working model and the \(m\)-th external trained model.

Integration of multiple external models

Suppose \(K\) external studies each provide a trained model with estimated coefficients \(\check{\mathbf{\beta}}_{1}, \dots, \check{\mathbf{\beta}}_{K}\). In principle, the original BRIER framework can be extended to incorporate multiple sources of external information by introducing one tuning parameter for each external model. However, the resulting hyperparameter space grows exponentially with \(K\), substantially increasing the computational burden of tuning parameter selection. To address this challenge, we propose a simple ensemble-based strategy that first combines the \(K\) external models into a single composite external model, which is then integrated with the local target data through the corresponding BRIER objective.

BRIERi.multi

When individual-level local data \(\{\mathbf{X}, \mathbf{Y}\}\) are available, each external model \(\check{\mathbf{\beta}}_{k}\) induces a model-implied linear predictor

\[ \mathbf{\nu}_{k} = \mathbf{X}\check{\mathbf{\beta}}_{k}, \]

and the corresponding mean response

\[ \check{\mathbf{\mu}}_{k} = g^{-1}(\mathbf{\nu}_{k}), \]

where \(g(\cdot)\) is the link function of the generalized linear model.

To combine the \(K\) external models, BRIERi.multi constructs an ensemble estimator by linearly aggregating their implied linear predictors. Specifically, we estimate ensemble weights \(\{\omega_k\}_{k=1}^K\) by fitting

\[ g(\mathbf{Y}) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \omega_k\, \mathbf{\nu}_{k}. \]

This yields a set of data-driven weights that reflect the relative contribution of each external model to the local prediction task.

The resulting composite external model is defined as the weighted combination of the external coefficient vectors:

\[ \check{\mathbf{\beta}} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \omega_k\, \check{\mathbf{\beta}}_{k}. \]

This aggregated coefficient vector can then be directly incorporated into the BRIER.I objective, reducing the dimensionality of the hyperparameter space while retaining predictive information across all external sources.

BRIERs.multi

When only summary-level local data are available, the individual-level quantities \(\{\mathbf{X}, \mathbf{Y}\}\) cannot be accessed. Instead, we assume availability of the summary-level proxy statistics

\[ \mathbf{r} = \frac{1}{n}\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{Y}, \qquad \mathbf{R} = \frac{1}{n}\mathbf{X}^{\top}\mathbf{X}, \]

where \(\mathbf{r}\) denotes the score vector and \(\mathbf{R}\) denotes the LD (correlation) matrix estimated from the local data.

In this setting, BRIERs.multi extends the ensemble framework of BRIERi.multi by estimating the ensemble weights using a least-squares proxy objective that depends only on \(\mathbf{r}\) and \(\mathbf{R}\). Let

\[ \mathbf{B} = \left( \check{\mathbf{\beta}}_{1}, \ldots, \check{\mathbf{\beta}}_{K} \right) \]

denote the matrix collecting the external coefficient vectors, and let \(\mathbf{w}\) denote the ensemble weights. Consider the proxy quadratic loss

\[ \frac{1}{n}\big\|\mathbf{Y} - \mathbf{X}\mathbf{B}\mathbf{w}\big\|_2^2. \]

Expanding this expression using only \(\mathbf{r}\) and \(\mathbf{R}\) yields

\[ \frac{1}{n}\|\mathbf{Y} - \mathbf{X}\mathbf{B}\mathbf{w}\|_2^2 = \text{const} - 2\,\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{B}^{\top}\mathbf{r} + \mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{B}^{\top}\mathbf{R}\mathbf{B}\mathbf{w}. \]

Setting the derivative to zero gives the closed-form estimator of the ensemble weights:

\[ \widehat{\mathbf{w}} = \big(\mathbf{B}^{\top}\mathbf{R}\mathbf{B}\big)^{-1} \mathbf{B}^{\top}\mathbf{r}. \]

The summary-level composite external model is then obtained as

\[ \check{\mathbf{\beta}} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \widehat{w}_k\,\check{\mathbf{\beta}}_{k}. \]

This aggregated coefficient vector can be plugged directly into the BRIER.S objective, enabling information borrowing from multiple external models without requiring individual-level local data or an independent validation set. BRIERs.multi therefore provides a computationally efficient and privacy-preserving approach for multi-study integration under summary-level constraints.

On this page
